coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as type II photosensitizers for photooxidation reactions of thioanisoles and styrenes
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Rui-Zhi
Shi, Xiao-Han
Wang, Xiao-Dong
Niu, Kai-Kai
Liu, Hui
Yu, Sheng-Sheng
Xing, Ling-Bao
coredata.dc:description: How to realize the selective regulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in carbon dots (CDs) and their application in photocatalytic reactions remains a challenge. In this study, the solvothermal synthesis method was employed to fabricate a novel class of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by utilizing vitamin B2 (VB) as the precursor. The resulting N-CDs exhibited notable efficiency in generating singlet oxygen (1O2). The N-CDs were employed as photocatalysts and shown remarkable photocatalytic efficiency in the photooxidation reactions of thioanisole and styrene, yielding 91 % and 92 %, respectively. Simultaneously, the photocatalysts have broad applicability in the context of photocatalytic conversion of thioanisole and styrene derivatives with high yields. This study not only synthesized N-CDs with controlled ROS generation, but also demonstrated their efficiency in several photocatalytic oxidation reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Singlet oxygen
Photooxidation
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the preparation of N-CDs.

(a), (b) TEM images of N-CDs, Inset: particle size of N-CDs; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs in the aqueous solution; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the aqueous solution. [N-CDs]=100 µg/mL.
(a) Controlled experiments of photooxidation of styrenes in the presence of different scavengers; (b) Proposed mechanism for photooxidation of thioanisoles and styrenes of N-CDs.

Control experiments of photooxidation of thioanisole under different conditions. a
Image, table 1 .

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: thioanisole (14 mg, 0.1 mmol), N-CDs as photocatalysts (7.5 mg), 390–400 nm LED (10 W), room temperature (RT), air, 2 h.

,

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: styrene derivatives (0.2 mmol), N-CDs as photocatalysts (7.5 mg), 390–400 nm LED (10 W), room temperature (RT), air, 36 h.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as type II photosensitizers for photooxidation reactions of thioanisoles and styrenes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: How to realize the selective regulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in carbon dots (CDs) and their application in photocatalytic reactions remains a challenge. In this study, the solvothermal synthesis method was employed to fabricate a novel class of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by utilizing vitamin B2 (VB) as the precursor. The resulting N-CDs exhibited notable efficiency in generating singlet oxygen (1O2). The N-CDs were employed as photocatalysts and shown remarkable photocatalytic efficiency in the photooxidation reactions of thioanisole and styrene, yielding 91 % and 92 %, respectively. Simultaneously, the photocatalysts have broad applicability in the context of photocatalytic conversion of thioanisole and styrene derivatives with high yields. This study not only synthesized N-CDs with controlled ROS generation, but also demonstrated their efficiency in several photocatalytic oxidation reactions.
N-CDs have been synthesized with notable efficiency in generating singlet oxygen, which can be used as photocatalysts for photooxidation reactions of thioanisoles and styrenes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The topic of organic photochemistry is currently experiencing significant development within the realm of organic chemistry [1,2]. The field of study refers to the investigation of the structure of atoms and molecules in states of electronic excitation, as well as their corresponding physical and chemical characteristics. A photochemical reaction is a chemical reaction that use light as an excitation mechanism to investigate the excited state of molecules [3–6]. The synthesis approach aims to achieve cleanliness, energy efficiency, and conservation [7–10]. This innovation offers novel approaches, methodologies, and technologies for organic synthetic chemistry, hence introducing fresh perspectives to the advancement of contemporary organic synthesis [11–16]. Hence, the development of novel, highly efficient photocatalysts that can facilitate effective chemical conversions in solvents that are ecologically benign is of utmost importance.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel category of carbon-based zero-dimensional materials, characterized by a small size often measuring less than 10 nm in diameter [17–22]. Owing to their distinct luminescent characteristics, widespread accessibility, and favorable biocompatibility, they have found extensive applications in biosensing and imaging, sensors, nanomedicines, optoelectronic devices, drug delivery, and catalysis [23–42]. It is noteworthy that CDs possess unique optical properties that distinguish them from both fluorescent and conventional semiconductor quantum dots, which integrate the advantageous optical characteristics of quantum dots with the electronic properties inherent to carbon materials. Furthermore, CDs possess the capability to regulate the wavelength of fluorescence emission with efficiency and exhibit a high quantum yield of fluorescence, rendering them highly suitable for application as catalysts in photocatalysis. This method has found extensive application in the design and development of groundbreaking chemical processes, such as synthetic photosynthesis and organic transformations. The presence of reactive surface groups on light-absorbing CDs has created novel catalytic application opportunities, which enables the production of significant organic compounds through light-induced radical reactions, which can be carried out under mild operational parameters [43–46]. Therefore, the fabrication of CDs and their utilization in organic photocatalysis have garnered significant interest in recent years due to their environmentally friendly nature and absence of metal catalysts, which have potential as efficient nanophotocatalytic substrates for a diverse array of photocatalytic reactions [47–53]. For examples, Kang and coworkers reported carbon quantum dots modified with hydroxyl groups on the surface by an electrochemical etching method, which can directly be used as effective heterogeneous nanocatalysts for H-bond catalysis in aldol condensations [54]. Wu and coworkers successfully developed a highly efficient borylation method for diazonium salts in aqueous media, employing readily synthesized, strongly luminescent, and crystalline CDs that exhibited favourable compatibility with moderate to high conversion efficiency [51]. Nevertheless, the oversight of photoactive species of CDs in photocatalytic processes and their targeted application to specific photocatalytic reactions remain significant challenges that require immediate attention.

Riboflavin, generally referred to as vitamin B2 (VB), is a crucial micronutrient that is important for maintaining optimal health in the human body, which exhibits a high level of safety and non-toxicity [55]. However, its effectiveness as a photosensitizer is hindered by its extremely poor solubility in water. Fortunately, the preparation of VB onto CDs results in the partial retention of its optical properties, leading to significant improvements in both water solubility and fluorescence quantum yield. The inherent structural characteristics and notable responsiveness to visible light exhibited by CDs serve as a compelling impetus for our investigation into their potential as photocatalysts [56–60]. In the present study, we have effectively synthesized and characterized unique nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using VB as a precursor. The as-prepared N-CDs have a high capability for generating singlet oxygen (1O2) when exposed to visible light. The compound 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)-dimalonic acid (ABDA) was chosen as the reagent for detecting reactive oxygen species (ROS) and specifically for detecting the production of 1O2. The as-prepared N-CDs demonstrate photocatalytic activity through the generation of 1O2, enabling them to function as photocatalysts for the photooxidation reaction of thioanisoles and styrenes. The improvement of reaction yield is achieved through the optimization of many parameters such as reaction times, excitation wavelengths, and solvents. The present study offers significant contributions to the field of CDs by investigating their design and manufacture, as well as exploring their prospective uses in photocatalytic reactions (Scheme 1 ).

N-CDs were successfully were prepared using a conventional one-step hydrothermal method, using VB as a carbon source and ethylenediamine as a cross-linking agent, which was characterized by various testing methods including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and steady-state spectra (UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra). As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the TEM images exhibit that the CDs are uniform spherical nanoparticles with good dispersion, and the size of a single particle is about 3–4 nm with the lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, which are consistent with the lattice spacings of the (100) planes of graphite. To further investigate the photochemical properties of CDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were investigated as shown in Fig. 1c and d. The absorption range of the CDs is from 220 nm to 400 nm, with three main peaks at 235 nm, 305 nm and 356 nm, respectively. Upon photoexcitation at the absorption maximum, CDs emits bright green fluorescence with peak at 502 nm.

Based on the findings presented in the literature article [55], it was observed that N-CDs exhibited a significantly elevated quantum yield of singlet oxygen (1O2) when dispersed in water. Consequently, we proceeded to investigate the ability of N-CDs to generate 1O2 in an aqueous solution by employing 9,10-anthracenediylbis(methylene) dimalonic acid (ABDA) as a probe for 1O2. The compound ABDA exhibits the ability to undergo a selective reaction with 1O2, leading to a consequent reduction in its absorbance. The absorption peak of ABDA in aqueous solutions of N-CDs was seen to diminish rapidly when exposed to purple light (390–400 nm, 10 W) for durations ranging from 0 to 30 s at ambient temperature. This observation suggests that N-CDs have the capability to successfully generate singlet oxygen 1O2. Furthermore, the rate of 1O2 production by N-CDs was found to be notably higher, as depicted in Fig. S1 and S2. In addition, upon the addition of TMPD to the N-CDs solution and subsequent irradiation under 390-400 nm LED for 1 min, there was no observable change in the UV-vis absorption spectra (in Fig. S2b), which suggested that N-CDs has no ability to produce O2 •–.

To advance the investigation into the utilization of N-CDs, our research endeavors involved the implementation of N-CDs as photosensitizers to enhance the photooxidation reactions of thioanisole. The excitation light source chosen for this study was visible light. The photooxidation processes were conducted at ambient temperature for a period of 2 h. The reactions took place in mixed solvents consisting of H2O and MeCN. As shown in Table 1 , the photocatalytic oxidation reaction of thioanisole demonstrates a high level of efficiency, with a yield of 91 % (entry 1, Fig. S3). When the photocatalyst is lowered to 2.5 mg and 5 mg, the corresponding reaction yields are reduced to 49 % and 70 %, respectively (entry 2 and 3). When the amount of photocatalyst is increased to 10 mg, there is a slight rise in the reaction yield to 93 % (entry 4). The reaction yields are observed to decrease to 54 % and 69 % (entry 5 and 6) when the water to acetonitrile ratio is 1:1 or 1:2, respectively. At the same time, reducing the reaction time to 1 h resulted in a significant decrease in the reaction yield (50%, entry 7), while increasing the reaction time to 4 h resulted in no observed changes in the reaction yield (92%, entry 8). The modification of the reaction solvents, specifically the substitution of water and acetonitrile with water (H2O), acetonitrile (MeCN), tetrahydrofuran (THF), or acetone (ACE), will lead to different levels of reduction in the reaction yields (entries 9–12), with yields of 57 %, 43 %, 39 %, and 36 %, respectively. We have also performed the photooxidation reactions of thioanisole under 365-367 nm light, which exhibited a yield of 81% (entry 13). In addition, the reaction does not occur in the absence of light (entry 14), indicating that light is a necessary condition for the reaction.

To ascertain the universality of N-CDs as photosensitizers, an investigation was conducted on the photooxidation reactions of thioanisole derivatives. The reactions were carried out under the ideal conditions outlined in Table 2 . The derivatives of thioanisole with para-substituting groups were synthesized with high yields (92 % for 2b, 90 % for 2c, 89 % for 2d, 90 % for 2e, 93 % for 2f, 92 % for 2 g, 95 % for 2 h, 86 % for 2i, and 92 % for 2j, Fig. S4-S12). Similarly, the ortho-substituting groups were also obtained with high yields (81 % for 2k, 77 % for 2l, and 76 % for 2 m, Fig. S13-S15) using the same reaction conditions. In addition, a significant yield of 73 % (2n, Fig. S16) was achieved in the synthesis of phenyl ethyl sulfoxide. In brief, photooxidation processes can be effectively achieved by both thioanisole derivatives with electron-withdrawing groups and those with electron-donating groups, resulting in high yields.

Inspired by the superior photocatalytic performance of N-CDs in photooxidation reactions of thioanisoles, we further applied the N-CDs as photocatalysts for photooxidation reaction of styrenes, in which 1,1-diphenylethylene was used as a template substrate. Firstly, we screened the proportion and type of solvents, the reaction times and the amount of photocatalysts. As shown in Table 3 , the photocatalytic oxidation reaction of 1,1-diphenylethylene can occur efficiently and give a yield of 92 % after 36 h (entry 1, Fig. S17). If the photocatalyst is reduced to 2.5 mg and 5 mg, the reaction yields are reduced to 51 % and 72 % (entries 2 and 3). If the photocatalyst is increased to 10 mg, the reaction yield is slightly increased to 94 % (entry 4). If the reation times are reduced to 12 h or 24 h, the reaction yields are reduced to 56 % and 80 % (entries 5 and 6), respectively. When the ratio of water to acetonitrile is 1:1 or 1:2, the reaction yields are reduced to 52 % and 72 % (entries 7 and 8), respectively. The alteration of the reaction solvents from water and acetonitrile to water (H2O), acetonitrile (MeCN), tetrahydrofuran (THF), or acetone (ACE) will result in varying degrees of reduction in the reaction yields (entries 9–12, 60 %, 42 %, 38 %, and 24 %). Moreover, control experiments revealed that the reaction failed to happen in the absence of catalysts, as well as when no light was presented, which means that both photocatalyst and light are essential prerequisites for the reaction to proceed effectively (entries 13 and 14).

To evaluate the applicability of N-CDs as a photocatalyst for the enhancement of photooxidation of styrene and its derivatives, various substrates were employed for the photooxidation reactions. As shown in Table 4 , styrene could be successfully oxidized to benzaldehyde (4b, Fig. S18) in 94 % yield under standard conditions. In addition, styrene derivatives containing electron-withdrawing groups (-F, -Cl, -Br) also showed successful reactions with high yields (90 % for 4c, 93 % for 4d, and 81 % for 4e, Fig. S19-S21). The substrate containing alkynyl substituent also give a high yield of 83 % (4f, Fig. S22). Similarly, the system demonstrated high efficiency in facilitating reactions involving substrates containing electron-donating groups (-CH3 and OCH3–), in which the yields of substrates were found to be 88 % (4 g, Fig. S23) and 79 % (4 h, Fig. S24), respectively. Substrate containing tert‑butyl substituent also exhibited excellent yield of 83 % (4i, Fig. S25). In addition, N-CDs also has high photocatalytic efficiencies for isopropenylbenzene and its derivatives (78 % for 4j, 79 % for 4k, and 80 % for 4l, Fig. S26-S28). These results demonstrate the great potential of N-CDs in promoting photooxidation of styrene derivatives under mild conditions.

In order to explore the mechanism and active substances of the photooxidation reaction of styrene and its derivatives, we have employed four free radical scavengers 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), triethylamine (TEA), potassium iodide (KI) and sodium azide (NaN3) to capture the photogenerated superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−), hydroxyl radicals (OH•), holes (h+) and singlet oxygen (1O2). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the introduction of NaN3 resulted in a significant decrease in the yield of the 1,1-diphenylethylene photooxidation reaction, which was only 18 % of the yield. However, under the same reaction conditions, the addition of BQ, TEA, and KI had no significant effect on the reaction yield, indicating that 1O2 is the main active species in the photooxidation reaction of styrene and its derivatives. Based on the experimental results and previous literature, the reaction mechanism of the thioanisoles and styrenes photooxidation reaction was proposed. Under light irradiation, N-CDs is excited from the ground state N-CDs to the excited state N-CDs*. Subsequently, the energy of N-CDs* is transferred to oxygen, resulting in the generation of 1O2 and N-CDs. The substrate then interacts with 1O2 to form the intermediate substance I and substance II, and finally form the target products.

In summary, this study employed VB as the precursor and ethylenediamine as the linking agents to fabricate N-CDs with favorable water solubility, which was achieved through a one-step reaction utilizing solvothermal synthesis method. The resulting N-CDs exhibited exceptional luminescence properties and demonstrated proficient 1O2 generation capability. Consequently, these N-CDs proved to be highly efficient photocatalysts for facilitating photocatalytic organic reactions for the photocatalytic oxidation of thioanisole and its derivatives, as well as styrene and its derivatives, demonstrates a notable capacity for effective catalytic conversion with a high degree of generic applicability. This research presents novel prospects for the utilization of CDs as eco-friendly photosensitizers, thereby broadening the scope of potential applications for CDs materials in the realm of photocatalysis.

Synthesis of N-CDs: N-CDs were synthesized using a single-step solvothermal technique in an aqueous medium. A solution containing 300 mg of VB and 1.0 mL of ethylenediamine was prepared by dissolving them in 20 mL of ultrapure water. The resulting solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at a temperature of 180 ⁰C for a period of 6 h. Following the completion of the reaction, the solid impurities were eliminated using the process of centrifugation. Subsequently, the resulting product underwent filtration using a membrane filter with a pore size of 0.22 mm. The filtrate was then put into a dialysis bag and allowed to undergo dialysis for a period of 12 h. The liquid contained within the dialysis bag underwent the process of lyophilization in order to be prepared for future use.

Rui-Zhi Dong: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao-Han Shi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao-Dong Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Kai-Kai Niu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hui Liu: Resources, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Sheng-Sheng Yu: Resources, Data curation. Ling-Bao Xing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52205210), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB018, ZR2022QE033, and ZR2021QB049).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mcat.2023.113749.
